content.text: Key insightThe declared element (numin the prior example) is not an array index. Rather, it is assigned the value of the array element being iterated over.
The declared element (numin the prior example) is not an array index. Rather, it is assigned the value of the array element being iterated over.
Best practiceFavor range-based for loops over regular for-loops when traversing containers.
Favor range-based for loops over regular for-loops when traversing containers.
Best practiceUse type deduction (auto) with range-based for loops to have the compiler deduce the type of the array element.
Use type deduction (auto) with range-based for loops to have the compiler deduce the type of the array element.

list: Don’t use type deduction in your range-based for loop. If we’d explicitly specified the element type asstd::string_view, then when the array was later updated tostd::string, thestd::stringelements will implicitly convert tostd::string_view, which is no problem. If the array is updated to some other type that is not convertible, the compiler will error, and we can figure out what the appropriate thing to do is at that point. But if the element type is convertible, then the compiler will silently perform the conversion and we may not realize we’re doing something suboptimal.Useconst auto&instead ofautowhen using type deduction in your range-based for loop when you don’t want to work with copies. The performance penalty of accessing elements through a reference instead of by value is likely to be small, and this insulates us from potentially significant performance penalties down the road if the element type is later changed to something that is expensive to copy.

Useconst auto&instead ofautowhen using type deduction in your range-based for loop when you don’t want to work with copies. The performance penalty of accessing elements through a reference instead of by value is likely to be small, and this insulates us from potentially significant performance penalties down the road if the element type is later changed to something that is expensive to copy.

content.text: Best practiceFor range-based for loops, prefer to define the element type as:autowhen you want to modify copies of the elements.auto&when you want to modify the original elements.const auto&otherwise (when you just need to view the original elements).

For range-based for loops, prefer to define the element type as:autowhen you want to modify copies of the elements.auto&when you want to modify the original elements.const auto&otherwise (when you just need to view the original elements).

content.text: For advanced readersRange-based for loops won’t work with decayed C-style arrays. This is because a range-based for-loop needs to know the length of the array to know when traversal is complete, and decayed C-style arrays do not contain this information.Range-based for loops also won’t work with enumerations. We show a method for working around this in lesson17.6 -- std::array and enumerations.

We show a method for working around this in lesson17.6 -- std::array and enumerations.
Range-based for loops also won’t work with enumerations. We show a method for working around this in lesson17.6 -- std::array and enumerations.

list: A function template that is using template argument deduction (when template type arguments are not explicitly specified) will not do conversions to match the template type parameters. The call either matches the template (and the template type can be deduced) or it doesn’t.A function template with an explicitly specified template type argument will convert arguments to match the parameter type (since the type is known).
A function template with an explicitly specified template type argument will convert arguments to match the parameter type (since the type is known).
